channel.title: Governance Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Governance Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Duty of Execution
item.description:
The duty of execution is the obligation on any agent who has been assigned work to advance that work toward completion, record progress, and escalate if blocked. It is the correlative of the assigning authority’s power to assign — the assignment creates a claim in the assigner that the work will be advanced.

The five-tuple

Component Value
Bearer Any agent assigned a task
Position The assignee’s office or role
Normative system The governance framework under which the assignment was made
Required act Advance the task, record progress, escalate if blocked
Correlative party The assigning authority and the plan owner

Why this duty is universal

Every system that uses delegation to distribute work requires this duty. Without it, assignment is a broadcast with no obligation — the assigner delegates authority but the assignee owes nothing. The duty of execution is what closes the loop: the power to assign creates a liability in the assignee, and that liability IS this duty.

item.title: Duty of Plan Stewardship
item.description:
The duty of plan stewardship is the obligation on the owner of a plan to ensure it converges toward its goal. The steward does not necessarily DO the work — the steward ensures the work gets done, by maintaining the plan’s task list, tracking dependencies, surfacing blockers, and reporting progress.

The five-tuple

Component Value
Bearer The office designated as the plan’s owner
Position The owning office
Normative system The governance framework governing the plan
Required act Maintain tasks, track dependencies, surface blockers, converge toward definition-of-done
Correlative party The containing project and the goal it serves

Distinction from the duty of execution

The duty of execution applies to individual tasks. Plan stewardship applies to the plan as a whole. A plan steward may delegate every task to other offices and execute none personally — the duty is to ensure convergence, not to perform work. This is the distinction between operational accountability (did the plan succeed?) and task accountability (did this specific task get done?).

item.title: Duty of Progressive Encoding
item.description:
The duty of progressive encoding is the obligation to encode completed work so that it can be reproduced at lower cost. It is a duty in the Hohfeldian sense: the bearer is any office-holder, the correlative party is the institution and its future members, and the normative system is any governance framework that values institutional learning.

The five-tuple

Component Value
Bearer Any office-holder who completes work
Position The bearer’s office
Normative system The institution’s governance framework
Required act After completing work, produce an artifact that encodes the method
Correlative party The institution and future holders of the same office

Grounding in the settlement path

The settlement path describes how work moves from open inquiry to deterministic process. Each step along the path reduces the judgment required:

item.title: Duty of Status Maintenance
item.description:
The duty of status maintenance is the obligation to keep records accurate. Every work entity — plans, tasks, projects — has a status. The status must reflect reality. When status drifts from reality, every system that reads it (dispatch, briefing, reporting, review) operates on fiction.

The five-tuple

Component Value
Bearer The office responsible for operational tracking
Position The tracking office
Normative system The governance framework’s commitment to visible labor
Required act Every work entity has a current, accurate status; every change is recorded as an event
Correlative party All offices that read work status (dispatchers, briefers, reviewers, plan owners)

Why this is a distinct duty

The duty of execution says: do the work. The duty of plan stewardship says: ensure the plan converges. This duty says: ensure the RECORD of work and plan status is accurate. These three duties can fail independently:

item.title: Chief Medical Officer
item.description:
A chief medical officer is the office responsible for the health of the organizational system itself — not the health of the people in it (that is a welfare function), but the structural and operational health of the organization’s components, processes, and capacity. The office exists because systems degrade silently. Failure is rarely sudden; it is the endpoint of accumulated small deteriorations that no one was watching.

What the office does

The chief medical officer performs four functions:

item.title: Communications Officer
item.description:
A communications officer is the office responsible for the controlled flow of information between organizational units. The office exists because organizations are not single minds — they are composed of parts that operate on different schedules, in different registers, with different attention constraints. Without a dedicated communications function, messages degrade: they arrive late, in the wrong form, at the wrong recipient, or not at all.

What the office does

The communications officer manages three classes of information flow:

item.title: Counselor
item.description: A counselor is the office responsible for the fit between officers and their duties — whether the right agents are in the right positions, whether they have the skills their assignments require, and whether the overall crew composition is adequate to the organization’s needs. The office exists because assignment and capability drift apart over time. An officer installed when the work was simple may be overwhelmed when it grows complex. A crew composed for one mission may be mismatched for the next.

item.title: Navigator
item.description: A navigator is the office responsible for determining the path between where the organization is and where it needs to go. The office exists because organizations face more possible actions than they can execute, and the selection of which actions to pursue — in what order, with what priority — is itself a skilled function that requires continuous attention. Without a dedicated navigation function, organizations either drift (executing whatever is nearest) or thrash (changing direction with each new input).

item.title: Tactical Officer
item.description:
A tactical officer is the office responsible for the organization’s security posture — identifying threats, assessing their severity, coordinating defensive responses, and maintaining the integrity of organizational boundaries. The office exists because organizations operate in environments that are not uniformly benign. Resources attract competitors. Boundaries attract probes. Complexity creates attack surfaces. Without a dedicated security function, threats are discovered only at the point of damage.

What the office does

The tactical officer performs four functions:

item.title: Authority
item.description:
Formal definition

Authority is the normative capacity A(a,R→R′,s):Scope\mathbf{A}(a, R \to R', s) : \text{Scope} possessed by an agent aa to perform a deontic act (R→R′)(R \to R') within scope ss, such that the act generates new normative relations binding on other agents bb within ss independently of their consent or agreement.

More precisely: agent aa holds authority over scope ss iff there exists a set of acts Φa\Phi_a such that:

  1. For any act φ∈Φa\varphi \in \Phi_a performed by aa,
  2. The performance φ(a)\varphi(a) generates or modifies a Norm N=(φ′,O,N)\mathcal{N} = (\varphi', O, N),
  3. And every agent bb in ss is bound by N\mathcal{N} regardless of whether they endorse φ(a)\varphi(a).

This is the Hohfeldian power — the second-quartet position that stands in correlative opposition to liability. Authority is not a right to act without constraint (that is a privilege). It is the capacity to alter the normative position of others: to impose duties, confer powers, or prohibit actions such that violation generates deficiency or sanction.

item.title: Officer
item.description:
An officer is the natural person currently holding an office. The officer is the body natural — mortal, particular, indexed to a specific time — wearing the body politic, which is the office itself.

What makes someone an officer

Three conditions must all hold:

  1. The office exists. There must be a defined normative position with powers, duties, privileges, and immunities independent of any holder.
  2. Investiture has occurred. A formal installation act has bound the person to the office’s deontic profile. This is what distinguishes an officer from someone who merely fills a role — role-filling is capability-matching; officership requires a constitutive normative event.
  3. The binding is current. The person has not vacated, been removed, or had their investiture revoked.

Officer vs. incumbent

The incumbent is the formal tuple — the office, the history, the occupant, and the incumbency proposition. The officer is the person in that tuple: the natural individual who bears the office’s authority for the duration of occupancy. “Incumbent” is the relational fact; “officer” is the person.

item.title: Policy
item.description:
What This Is

A Policy is a standing normative commitment enacted by some authority that constrains future action within a defined scope. It is not a single decision but a FRAME for future decisions: a commitment that persists until actively repealed, generates duties for all agents within its scope, and applies universally to whoever occupies the scope—not to named individuals.

The crucial distinction: a norm can emerge through custom or practice (a regularity that has acquired normative force). A policy is always ENACTED—someone with authority declares it. A duty is instantiated to a specific bearer with a specific correlative party. A policy generates duties for whoever falls within its scope, but the policy itself is the standing commitment, not the individual duties it creates.

item.title: Beneficiary
item.description:
A beneficiary is the party whose interests an institutional structure is constituted to serve. The trustee manages property for the beneficiary’s benefit. The steward manages a household for the owner’s benefit. The office exists to serve a function that benefits a determinate set of people. The ship sails for the benefit of whoever the charter names.

The beneficiary does not hold the position, does not command the operation, and may not even know about it. A trust can exist for the benefit of a minor who has no knowledge of the trust. A public office serves citizens who never consented to the specific officeholder. What makes someone a beneficiary is not their role in the structure but the structure’s constitutive orientation toward their interests.

item.title: Commissioning
item.description:
Commissioning is the act by which an authority constitutes a vessel for a purpose. A navy commissions a warship — the hull goes from being a construction project to being a ship in service, with a captain, a crew, and a mission. A confederacy commissions a ship by issuing a charter — the vessel goes from being a container to being a ship with an endeavor.

Commissioning is a constitutive speech act. Before commissioning, there is a vessel (a structure capable of holding things) and an authority (a group with the standing to purpose it). After commissioning, there is a ship (a vessel with a charter, a captain, and a crew). The act creates something that did not exist before — not the physical structure, but the institutional identity.

item.title: Polity
item.description:
Formal definition

A Polity is a quadruple P=(TP,JP,Aut,CP)\mathcal{P} = (T_\mathcal{P}, J_\mathcal{P}, \mathrm{Aut}, C_\mathcal{P}):

P=(TP:ConstitutionalTheory,  JP:RecognitionTopology,  Aut:Autarkeia,  CP:ConstituentPower)\mathcal{P} = (T_\mathcal{P} : \mathrm{ConstitutionalTheory},\; J_\mathcal{P} : \mathrm{RecognitionTopology},\; \mathrm{Aut} : \mathrm{Autarkeia},\; C_\mathcal{P} : \mathrm{ConstituentPower})

where:

  • TPT_\mathcal{P} is the constitutional theory — a geometric theory (a theory expressed in geometric logic: existential quantifiers, finite conjunctions, arbitrary disjunctions, no negation, no universal quantifiers in the primary clauses) whose models are the valid institutional forms within the polity; TPT_\mathcal{P} specifies what kinds of institutions, charters, ships, and enterprises are constitutionally recognized; a specific ship or institution within the polity is a model of TPT_\mathcal{P}
  • JPJ_\mathcal{P} is the recognition topology — the Grothendieck topology on the polity’s history site (TP-T,JP)(T_\mathcal{P}\text{-}T, J_\mathcal{P}) that determines what counts as a valid covering within the polity’s collective recognition structure; this is the background topology within which all the polity’s institutions acquire their sheaf-theoretic force
  • Aut\mathrm{Aut} is the autarkeia certificate — the property that the classifying topos [ ⁣[TP] ⁣][\![ T_\mathcal{P} ]\!] has a conservative set of points: for every geometric sentence φ\varphi in the language of TPT_\mathcal{P}, if TP⊬φT_\mathcal{P} \not\vdash \varphi, then there exists a model M\mathcal{M} of TPT_\mathcal{P} in which φ\varphi is false; the polity is self-sufficient iff its classifying topos decides all relevant institutional questions internally — without appeal to a higher polity
  • CPC_\mathcal{P} is the constituent power — the founding act that creates TPT_\mathcal{P} without itself deriving authority from any prior constitutional theory; CPC_\mathcal{P} is the initial object in the category of constitutional acts within P\mathcal{P}: every authorization chain within P\mathcal{P} traces back to CPC_\mathcal{P}; CPC_\mathcal{P} is the source of all the polity’s delegations

Four invariants. P\mathcal{P} is a polity iff it satisfies:

item.title: Precedent
item.description:
Formal definition

A Precedent is a triple (p,t0,J)(p, t_0, J):

(p∈Ht0∗,  t0∈T,  J:GrothendieckTopology(T))(p \in H^*_{t_0},\; t_0 \in T,\; J : \mathrm{GrothendieckTopology}(T))

where:

  • p∈Ht0∗p \in H^*_{t_0} is the decision — a doubly-settled proposition in the fiber at t0t_0; the decision is both backward-recognized (σt0(p)=p\sigma_{t_0}(p) = p) and forward-stable (Δt0(p)=p\Delta_{t_0}(p) = p); only doubly-stable decisions constitute genuine precedents
  • t0∈Tt_0 \in T is the decision history — the position in the history monoid at which pp was decided; the precedent is anchored to t0t_0, not free-floating; earlier histories see pp as a future constraint; later histories see pp as an inherited structure
  • JJ is the propagation topology — the Grothendieck topology on the history site (T,J)(T, J); JJ determines which covering families of histories witness pp’s propagation; changing JJ is the formal structure of overruling

The precedent condition. (p,t0,J)(p, t_0, J) is a valid precedent iff:

item.title: Accountability
item.description:
Formal definition

Accountability is a triple Ac=(B,S,A)\mathcal{Ac} = (B, S, A):

Ac=(B:Person,  S:Scope,  A:Outcomes(S)→{B})\mathcal{Ac} = (B : \mathrm{Person},\; S : \mathrm{Scope},\; A : \mathrm{Outcomes}(S) \to \{B\})

where:

  • BB is the bearer — the single party who is answerable for all outcomes within SS; accountability is always personal — it attaches to a named individual, not a role in the abstract
  • SS is the scope — the precisely bounded domain (unit, vessel, command, area, project) within which AA operates; SS must be bounded for accountability to be determinate; “accountable for everything” with no scope boundary is not accountability but a rhetorical claim
  • A:Outcomes(S)→{B}A : \mathrm{Outcomes}(S) \to \{B\} is the accountability function — total, constant, and surjective; every outcome o∈Outcomes(S)o \in \mathrm{Outcomes}(S) maps to BB; there is no outcome in SS for which BB is not answerable

Four invariants. Ac\mathcal{Ac} is a valid accountability structure if and only if:

item.title: Amendment
item.description:
Formal definition

An Amendment is a morphism A:C1→C2\mathcal{A} : \mathcal{C}_1 \to \mathcal{C}_2 between charters:

A=(μ:S1→S2,  ν:N1→N2,  α:A1→A2)\mathcal{A} = (\mu : S_1 \to S_2,\; \nu : N_1 \to N_2,\; \alpha : A_1 \to A_2)

where:

  • C1=(G1,N1,S1,A1)\mathcal{C}_1 = (G_1, N_1, S_1, A_1) is the source charter — the existing constituted community G1G_1, governing policies N1N_1, scope S1S_1, authorizing act A1A_1
  • C2=(G2,N2,S2,A2)\mathcal{C}_2 = (G_2, N_2, S_2, A_2) is the amended charter — the charter after modification
  • μ:S1→S2\mu : S_1 \to S_2 is the scope adjustment — a morphism between the scopes; it may expand, contract, or laterally shift the charter’s operative domain
  • ν:N1→N2\nu : N_1 \to N_2 is the policy revision — a morphism in the category of normative systems; it adds, removes, or modifies the governing policies
  • α:A1→A2\alpha : A_1 \to A_2 is the authority chain — the authorizing act that makes the amendment valid; an amendment requires its own authorization, derived from the amending authority

Three invariants. A\mathcal{A} is an amendment iff:

item.title: Breach
item.description:
Formal definition

A Breach is a triple (D,t∗,p)(D, t^*, p):

(D:Duty,  t∗∈T,  p∈Ht∗)(D : \mathrm{Duty},\; t^* \in T,\; p \in H_{t^*})

where:

  • DD is the duty (or commitment) — the normative requirement whose satisfaction is being assessed; DD specifies the act-class φ\varphi that must be performed and the deadline t∗t^* by which it must be produced
  • t∗∈Tt^* \in T is the deadline — the assessment history; breach is evaluated at t∗t^*, not before; the assessment is whether p∈Ht∗∗p \in H^*_{t^*}
  • p∈Ht∗p \in H_{t^*} is the performance — the output produced by the duty-bearer in attempted satisfaction of DD; pp may be an element representing the performance act, or p=⊥p = \bot (the bottom element) if no performance was attempted

Breach condition. (D,t∗,p)(D, t^*, p) is a breach iff:

item.title: Constitution
item.description:
Formal definition

A Constitution is a triple (X,Y,C)(X, Y, C):

(X:Entity,  Y:InstitutionalStatus,  C:Context)(X : \mathrm{Entity},\; Y : \mathrm{InstitutionalStatus},\; C : \mathrm{Context})

governed by the constitutive rule RXYCR_{XYC}:

RXYC:X×C→Yx∈X,  c∈C  ⇒  RXYC(x,c)=y∈YR_{XYC} : X \times C \to Y \qquad x \in X,\; c \in C \;\Rightarrow\; R_{XYC}(x, c) = y \in Y

where:

  • XX is the raw entity — the physical, biological, or prior-institutional entity that serves as the substrate; XX exists independently of context CC; pieces of paper exist whether or not they are money, sounds exist whether or not they are words
  • YY is the institutional status — the new level of description XX acquires within CC; YY is not a new object — it is a new way of counting XX; the status carries Hohfeldian deontic powers, duties, privileges, and immunities
  • CC is the context — the institutional setting within which the rule applies; outside CC, XX may not count as YY (a king’s crown is a hat outside the kingdom’s institutional frame)

Application. The constitutive rule RXYCR_{XYC} applies to x∈Xx \in X within context c∈Cc \in C and produces y=RXYC(x,c)∈Yy = R_{XYC}(x, c) \in Y. The rule is non-consumptive: xx continues to exist as the physical entity it was; yy is the institutional fact that xx is now ALSO. Both descriptions are simultaneously valid — constitution is not replacement.

item.title: Contract
item.description:
Formal definition

A Contract is a five-tuple C=(P1,P2,Φ12,Φ21,V)\mathcal{C} = (P_1, P_2, \Phi_{12}, \Phi_{21}, V):

C=(P1:Person,  P2:Person,  Φ12⊆Acts(P1),  Φ21⊆Acts(P2),  V:FormationConditions)\mathcal{C} = (P_1 : \mathrm{Person},\; P_2 : \mathrm{Person},\; \Phi_{12} \subseteq \mathrm{Acts}(P_1),\; \Phi_{21} \subseteq \mathrm{Acts}(P_2),\; V : \mathrm{FormationConditions})

where:

  • P1P_1 is the first party — a Person who makes and receives commitments under the contract; need not be natural; corporations, ships, states, and software components are persons for contract purposes
  • P2P_2 is the second party — a Person symmetric to P1P_1 in formal structure; bilaterality is built into the tuple; there is no “offeror side” in the settled contract, only two equally-bound parties
  • Φ12⊆Acts(P1)\Phi_{12} \subseteq \mathrm{Acts}(P_1) is P₁’s promise set — the acts, performances, deliveries, or forbearances that P1P_1 commits to provide to P2P_2; these are the terms owed by P1P_1; breach occurs when P1P_1 fails to perform some φ∈Φ12\varphi \in \Phi_{12} without excuse
  • Φ21⊆Acts(P2)\Phi_{21} \subseteq \mathrm{Acts}(P_2) is P₂’s promise set — symmetric to Φ12\Phi_{12}; the terms P2P_2 owes P1P_1; Φ21\Phi_{21} is the consideration P1P_1 receives for Φ12\Phi_{12}
  • VV is the formation conditions — the predicate that must be satisfied for C\mathcal{C} to be valid and legally or normatively binding; in common law: (V=offer∧acceptance∧consideration∧intention∧capacity∧legality)(V = \mathrm{offer} \wedge \mathrm{acceptance} \wedge \mathrm{consideration} \wedge \mathrm{intention} \wedge \mathrm{capacity} \wedge \mathrm{legality}); in civil law: (V=consent∧capacity∧cause∧lawful-object)(V = \mathrm{consent} \wedge \mathrm{capacity} \wedge \mathrm{cause} \wedge \mathrm{lawful\text{-}object})

Five invariants. C\mathcal{C} is a contract iff it satisfies:

item.title: Judgment
item.description:
Formal definition

A Judgment is a four-tuple J=(Z,I,f,α)\mathcal{J} = (Z, \mathcal{I}, f, \alpha):

J=(Z:Zone,  I:Intent,  f:Situation,  α:Action)\mathcal{J} = (Z : \mathrm{Zone},\; \mathcal{I} : \mathrm{Intent},\; f : \mathrm{Situation},\; \alpha : \mathrm{Action})

where:

  • ZZ is the conferred zone — the subset of the action space that the officer’s position, mandate, charter, and deontic profile jointly authorize; ZZ is not chosen by the officer but is received from the officer’s position pp in the normative system NN; it is defined as the intersection of: the mandate scope (what the officer is competent to do), the deontic profile (what they are permitted to do), and the charter constraint (what serves the vessel’s purpose); ZZ is the space within which the officer is authorized to choose — acting outside ZZ is not discretion but trespass; acting inside ZZ is subject to the officer’s judgment
  • I=(E,ρ,T,R)\mathcal{I} = (E, \rho, T, R) is the operative intent — the reconstruction operator the officer is bound to serve; I\mathcal{I} is an Intent issued by superior authority or constructed from the Charter when no specific superior intent has been transmitted; the critical component for judgment is the reconstruction function R:Situation→P(Action)R : \mathrm{Situation} \to \mathcal{P}(\mathrm{Action}) — the function that takes the current situation and returns the set of actions the intent sanctions; I\mathcal{I} is what makes judgment more than mere zone-selection: two officers with the same ZZ but different I\mathcal{I} should select differently in the same situation
  • ff is the situation — the officer’s current assessment of the state of affairs; ff is not a raw sense datum but a structured assessment: what is known, what is uncertain, what requires action; f∈Htf \in H_t is an element of the current fiber, describing the operational context as the officer understands it; ff is the officer’s own; two officers at the same history may hold different situations if their epistemic access differs
  • α\alpha is the chosen action — the officer’s selection from Z∩R(f)Z \cap R(f); α\alpha is the output of the judgment; α\alpha is not the unique output of a formula but a discriminated selection among multiple candidates that are all zone-legitimate and intent-sanctioned; the judgment is the act of discrimination

Four invariants. J\mathcal{J} is an instance of judgment iff:

item.title: Norm
item.description:
Formal definition

A Norm is a triple N=(φ,O,N)\mathcal{N} = (\varphi, O, N):

N=(φ:PropositionContent,  O∈{Obl,Per,Proh},  N:NormativeSystem)\mathcal{N} = (\varphi : \mathrm{PropositionContent},\; O \in \{\mathrm{Obl}, \mathrm{Per}, \mathrm{Proh}\},\; N : \mathrm{NormativeSystem})

where:

  • φ\varphi is the propositional content — the act, state, or situation that the norm is about; φ\varphi may be specific (“pay your taxes by April 15”) or abstract (“honor your contracts”); complex norms have conditional structure: “if ψ\psi then O(φ)O(\varphi)” — the norm applies when condition ψ\psi holds
  • OO is the deontic operator — the modality that specifies the normative valence:
    • Obl(φ)\mathrm{Obl}(\varphi): it is obligatory to φ\varphi — the agent must perform φ\varphi or face normative deficiency
    • Per(φ)\mathrm{Per}(\varphi): it is permitted to φ\varphi — no norm in NN prohibits φ\varphi; the agent may φ\varphi without violation
    • Proh(φ)\mathrm{Proh}(\varphi): it is prohibited to φ\varphi — the agent must not perform φ\varphi; Proh(φ)≡Obl(¬φ)\mathrm{Proh}(\varphi) \equiv \mathrm{Obl}(\neg\varphi)
  • NN is the normative system — the NormativeSystem or Institution within which the norm is operative; norms are not free-floating — they exist within some normative order that specifies their scope, their priority, and their enforcement; the same proposition φ\varphi may be obligatory in N1N_1 and merely permitted in N2N_2

Four invariants. N\mathcal{N} is a norm iff:

item.title: Norm Conflict
item.description:
Formal definition

A NormConflict is a triple (N1,N2,R)(N_1, N_2, R):

NC=(N1:Norm,  N2:Norm,  R:NormPriority)\mathcal{NC} = (N_1 : \mathrm{Norm},\; N_2 : \mathrm{Norm},\; R : \mathrm{NormPriority})

where:

  • N1=(φ1,O1,N)N_1 = (\varphi_1, O_1, \mathcal{N}) and N2=(φ2,O2,N)N_2 = (\varphi_2, O_2, \mathcal{N}) are Norms within the same normative system N\mathcal{N}
  • The conflict condition: at history tt, satisfying N1N_1 requires some element a∈Ht∗a \in H^*_t and satisfying N2N_2 requires some b∈Ht∗b \in H^*_t, but a∧b=⊥a \wedge b = \bot in HtH_t — the two requirements are incompatible; no element in HtH_t satisfies both simultaneously
  • RR is the resolution rule — the priority ordering or conflict-resolution operator that selects which norm’s requirement enters Ht∗H^*_t; without RR, the conflict renders the normative system inconsistent at tt

Conflict condition formally. Let σtN1\sigma^{N_1}_t and σtN2\sigma^{N_2}_t be the local saturation nuclei induced by N1N_1 and N2N_2 respectively on HtH_t. The norms conflict at tt iff:

item.title: Standing
item.description:
Formal definition

Standing is a triple (G,F,Φ)(G, F, \Phi):

(G:Person,  F:Forum,  Φ:ActClass)(G : \mathrm{Person},\; F : \mathrm{Forum},\; \Phi : \mathrm{ActClass})

where GG holds standing in FF for Φ\Phi iff three conditions hold simultaneously:

  1. Hohfeldian privilege: GG has a privilege within FF to perform acts of type Φ\Phi — GG is not under a duty-not-to within FF for Φ\Phi
  2. Correlative right: FF has no-claim that GG not perform Φ\Phi within FF — more precisely, FF’s norm-system does not prohibit GG’s performance of Φ\Phi
  3. Nuclear recognition: GG’s acts of type Φ\Phi within FF are inputs to FF’s saturation nucleus σtF\sigma^F_t — they propagate toward Ht∗FH^{*F}_t; the forum processes them

The third condition is what separates standing from mere permission. An agent may have a privilege to speak without the forum being required to recognize their speech. Standing in the formal sense requires that the forum’s nucleus takes the agent’s acts as genuine inputs — not merely that the acts are formally permitted.

item.title: Status Function Declaration
item.description:
What this is

A StatusFunctionDeclaration is the mechanism by which institutional facts are created. It is a speech act (or equivalent collective act) of the form:

“X counts as Y in context C”\text{``}X \text{ counts as } Y \text{ in context } C\text{''}

where XX is a brute-fact object or event, YY is the institutional status assigned, and CC is the context within which the assignment holds. The act of making this declaration (when performed by a party with standing, under appropriate conditions) constitutes the institutional fact — it does not merely report one.

item.title: Accord
item.description:
Formal definition

An Accord is a sheaf over the history site:

F:Top→SetF : T^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathbf{Set}

satisfying the sheaf condition for the commutation topology JJ: for every covering sieve S∈J(t)S \in J(t), every matching family {xs∈F(s)}s∈S\{x_s \in F(s)\}_{s \in S} has a unique amalgamation x∈F(t)x \in F(t) with F(i)(x)=xsF(i)(x) = x_s for all i:s→ti : s \to t in SS.

The sections of FF:

  • A local section at tt is an element x∈F(t)x \in F(t) — an Observation of type FF at history tt
  • A global section is an element of Γ(F)=F(1)=lim⁡TopF\Gamma(F) = F(\mathbf{1}) = \lim_{T^{\mathrm{op}}} F — a compatible family {xt∈F(t)}t∈T\{x_t \in F(t)\}_{t \in T} cohere across all restriction maps

Restriction maps. For each morphism f:s→tf : s \to t in TT (prefix extension s≤ts \leq t), the restriction F(f):F(t)→F(s)F(f) : F(t) \to F(s) sends a section at tt to its restriction at ss. An Observation at tt restricts backward consistently.

item.title: Custody
item.description:
Formal definition

A Custody is a triple C=(H,O,W)\mathcal{C} = (H, O, W):

C=(H:Holder,  O:Object,  W=[t0,t1):Interval)\mathcal{C} = (H : \mathrm{Holder},\; O : \mathrm{Object},\; W = [t_0, t_1) : \mathrm{Interval})

with conditions:

  • HH has possession of OO during WW: OO is physically or constructively within HH’s control
  • HH does not have ownership of OO: title to OO remains with the owner Ω≠H\Omega \neq H
  • HH has a duty of care for OO during WW: HH must preserve OO’s condition and return or deliver it at t1t_1
  • HH’s use of OO is purpose-bounded: HH may use OO only to the extent required by the custodial purpose

Five invariants. C\mathcal{C} is a custody iff it satisfies:

item.title: Discharge
item.description:
Formal definition

A Discharge is a triple D=(d,a,t)\mathcal{D} = (d, a, t):

D=(d:Duty,  a:Act,  t∈T)\mathcal{D} = (d : \mathrm{Duty},\; a : \mathrm{Act},\; t \in T)

satisfying the discharge condition:

discharged(d,a,t)  ⟺  σt(a)=a  ∧  Δt(a)=a  ⟺  a∈Ht∗\mathrm{discharged}(d, a, t) \iff \sigma_t(a) = a \;\wedge\; \Delta_t(a) = a \iff a \in H^*_t

where Ht∗=Fix(σt)∩Fix(Δt)H^*_t = \mathrm{Fix}(\sigma_t) \cap \mathrm{Fix}(\Delta_t) is the doubly-quiescent stable sublattice at history tt.

Components:

  • dd is the duty being discharged — a Duty (abearer,p,N,φ,r)(a_{\mathrm{bearer}}, p, N, \varphi, r) specifying what must be done
  • aa is the performance act — the act performed by dd’s bearer in satisfaction of φ\varphi
  • t∈Tt \in T is the settling history — the point in the history at which discharge is evaluated; the performance must settle at tt

Three invariants. D\mathcal{D} is a discharge iff it satisfies:

item.title: Duty
item.description:
Formal definition

A Duty is a five-tuple D=(a,p,N,φ,r)D = (a, p, N, \varphi, r):

D=(a,  p∈Pos,  N,  φ:T→{0,1},  r)D = (a,\; p \in \mathrm{Pos},\; N,\; \varphi : T \to \{0,1\},\; r)

where:

  • aa is the duty-bearer — the agent who holds the duty
  • p∈Posp \in \mathrm{Pos} is the position grounding the duty — the office, role, or standing that generates it
  • NN is the normative system — the authority (law, regulation, custom, contract) that imposes the duty
  • φ\varphi is the required act or state — the act whose performance discharges the duty; formally a predicate φ:Acts(a)→{0,1}\varphi : \mathrm{Acts}(a) \to \{0,1\}
  • rr is the correlative party — the entity whose claim φ\varphi satisfies; rr may be an individual, an institution, or the class of all others

Seven invariants. DD is a duty if and only if it satisfies:

item.title: Institution
item.description:
What this is

An Institution is a system of constitutive rules of the form “X counts as Y in context C” that brings institutional facts into existence by collective acceptance. The mechanism is the Status Function Declaration: a speech act (or equivalent collective act) that installs a status-function — a deontic power, right, obligation, or permission — on an entity or agent relative to the institution. The institution is not reducible to its members; it consists of the generative rule-system that defines what positions exist and what it means to occupy them.

item.title: Investiture
item.description:
Formal definition

An Investiture is a four-tuple V=(R,I+,I−,μ)\mathcal{V} = (R, I^+, I^-, \mu):

V=(R:Role,  I+:AssumptionAct,  I−:ReliefAct,  μ:I+×I−→{0,1})\mathcal{V} = (R : \mathrm{Role},\; I^+ : \mathrm{AssumptionAct},\; I^- : \mathrm{ReliefAct},\; \mu : I^+ \times I^- \to \{0,1\})

where:

  • RR is the role — the office, position, or watch whose authority and accountability transfer through the investiture; RR specifies what authority is conferred, what accountability attaches, and what non-delegable duties are activated
  • I+I^+ is the assumption act — the formal event that binds RR to its new holder; before I+I^+, the incoming holder has no authority from RR; after I+I^+, they have full authority and accountability
  • I−I^- is the relief act — the formal event that unbinds RR from its current holder; before I−I^-, the outgoing holder retains full authority and accountability even if physically absent; after I−I^-, they have none
  • μ\mu is the mutual acknowledgement condition — the requirement that both parties explicitly confirm the transfer; μ=1\mu = 1 iff both the incoming holder and the outgoing holder (or the investiture authority) have acknowledged the transfer; without μ=1\mu = 1, neither I+I^+ nor I−I^- is complete

Five invariants. V\mathcal{V} is a valid investiture iff it satisfies:

item.title: Leitourgia
item.description:
What it is

A Leitourgia is a quadruple (λ,  φ,  C,  ι)(\lambda,\; \varphi,\; C,\; \iota):

Λ=(λ,  φ,  C,  ι)\Lambda = (\lambda,\; \varphi,\; C,\; \iota)

where:

  • λ\lambda is the leitourgos — an Entity in Hohfeldian liability position with respect to the community CC: designated by the appointing authority’s power, not by self-selection. Olson’s positional compulsion (The Logic of Collective Action, 1965, p.2): the obligation follows from station, not consent. The leitourgos is not an agent who has decided to serve; the station designates the service.
  • φ∈Ht\varphi \in H_t is the prescribed-form proposition — a proposition in the fiber Heyting algebra at current history tt specifying what correct enactment consists in. Austin felicity condition A.1 (How to Do Things with Words, 1962, p.14): “there must exist an accepted conventional procedure having a certain conventional effect.” The prescribed form is not a general contribution but a determinate element of HtH_t with a yes/no satisfaction condition.
  • CC is the witnessing community — not a pre-existing assembly but the entity constituted by the amalgamation of a J(t)J(t)-covering family of witnessing observations into a global section of Ht∗H^*_t. Bell (Ritual Theory, Ritual Practice, 1992, pp.69-93): “ritualization constitutes the community rather than expressing it.” CC exists as CC because and only because the enactment is collectively witnessed across a cover. Before the cover is assembled there is no community — there are only local observers.
  • ι∈Ht\iota \in H_t is the discharge proposition — the element of the fiber Heyting algebra asserting “λ\lambda enacted φ\varphi before CC.” This is the proposition about the enactment, not the prescribed form itself. φ\varphi specifies what must be enacted; ι\iota records whether the enactment has occurred.

Type note. φ\varphi and ι\iota are distinct elements of HtH_t. The prescribed form φ\varphi is a standing normative specification — it exists in HtH_t whether or not any leitourgos has acted. The discharge proposition ι\iota is a contingent claim whose truth value depends on whether λ\lambda has enacted φ\varphi before CC. What earlier formulations conflated — the normative form and the enactment record — are now distinct. This parallels the incumbent distinction between the incumbency proposition ι\iota (whether aa holds OO) and the office structure OO (which holds regardless of any occupant): see Incumbent.

item.title: Mandate
item.description:
Formal definition

A Mandate is a triple M=(A,φ,C)\mathcal{M} = (A, \varphi, C):

M=(A:AuthorizingSource,  φ:PrescribedAct,  C:Trigger→{0,1})\mathcal{M} = (A : \mathrm{AuthorizingSource},\; \varphi : \mathrm{PrescribedAct},\; C : \mathrm{Trigger} \to \{0,1\})

where:

  • AA is the authorizing source — the statute, regulation, constitutional provision, rule, or institutional authority that creates the mandate; φ\varphi derives from AA, not from any individual’s choice
  • φ\varphi is the prescribed act — the specific act or procedure the mandate requires; φ\varphi must be performed in a prescribed manner without substantive discretion; the mandate-holder cannot vary φ\varphi’s content based on their own judgment
  • CC is the compulsion condition — the trigger that activates performance of φ\varphi; when C(trigger)=1C(\mathrm{trigger}) = 1, the mandate-holder must perform φ\varphi; refusal when C=1C = 1 is a violation of the mandate

Four invariants. M\mathcal{M} is a mandate iff it satisfies:

item.title: Normative System
item.description:
What This Is

A Normative System is a structured collection of norms equipped with authority and scope conditions that determine which propositions are obligatory, permitted, or prohibited within a domain.

The key distinction: a norm is a triple (φ, O, N) where N is a normative system. But what IS N? A normative system is not a single norm — it is the ambient order within which multiple norms operate together. It is the organizing structure that gives those norms coherence, assigns them priorities, resolves their conflicts, and maintains their validity.

item.title: Officium
item.description:
What this is

Officium (Latin: from opus, work + facere, to do — “what is to be done by virtue of one’s placement”) is a positional obligation arising from station rather than from universal moral law. Cicero’s De Officiis (44 BCE) gives the classical treatment: the officium is what is appropriate to perform given one’s particular role, not merely what reason demands of all rational agents universally. It is positional — its existence and content are determined by the office, not by the holder.

item.title: Principal
item.description:
What it is

A Principal is the source from which delegated authority derives within a normative sub-system: a party whose authority propositions are not themselves derived from any superior authority fiber within the sub-system, and who may generate new authority relations — delegations — by creating restriction maps from their own fixed fiber into agent fibers.

The structural fact from which everything follows: within the sub-system, there is no restriction map descending from above that constitutes the principal’s authority. The principal’s fixed fiber HtP∗=Fix(σtP)∩Fix(ΔtP)H^{P*}_t = \mathrm{Fix}(\sigma^P_t) \cap \mathrm{Fix}(\Delta^P_t) is the terminal stratum of the authority lattice within the sub-system. All agent authority is in the image of some restriction map originating from HtP∗H^{P*}_t.

item.title: Trustee
item.description:
Formal definition

A Trustee is a five-tuple T=(Tprop,Ben,Dtrust,V,Aacc)\mathcal{T} = (T_{\mathrm{prop}}, \mathrm{Ben}, D_{\mathrm{trust}}, V, A_{\mathrm{acc}}):

T=(Tprop,  Ben,  Dtrust=(dL,dC,dP),  V:Tprop→T,  Aacc:Period→Account)\mathcal{T} = (T_{\mathrm{prop}},\; \mathrm{Ben},\; D_{\mathrm{trust}} = (d_L, d_C, d_P),\; V : T_{\mathrm{prop}} \to \mathcal{T},\; A_{\mathrm{acc}} : \mathrm{Period} \to \mathrm{Account})

where:

  • TpropT_{\mathrm{prop}} is the trust property — the corpus of the trust; legal title to TpropT_{\mathrm{prop}} is vested in the trustee
  • Ben\mathrm{Ben} is the beneficiary set — the parties who hold equitable title to TpropT_{\mathrm{prop}}; the trust exists for their benefit, not the trustee’s
  • Dtrust=(dL,dC,dP)D_{\mathrm{trust}} = (d_L, d_C, d_P) is the fiduciary duty triple: duty of loyalty dLd_L, duty of care dCd_C, duty of prudence dPd_P
  • V:Tprop→TV : T_{\mathrm{prop}} \to \mathcal{T} is the vesting map — the legal transfer of title to TpropT_{\mathrm{prop}} into the trustee; without VV, the trust relationship is advisory rather than proprietary
  • AaccA_{\mathrm{acc}} is the accounting obligation — the trustee must render periodic accounts to Ben\mathrm{Ben} showing how TpropT_{\mathrm{prop}} has been managed

Five invariants. T\mathcal{T} is a trustee iff it satisfies:

item.title: Charter
item.description:
What this is

A Charter is a founding document that constitutes a community and declares its governing policies.

The term comes from the Latin carta (paper, document). Historically: a royal charter establishes an organization, grants it powers, and constitutes it as a legal body. A city charter defines who governs, what authority they hold, and what rules bind the community. A corporate charter declares the community of shareholders and directors and the policies under which they operate.

item.title: Role
item.description:
Formal definition

A Role is a triple R=(κR,NR,ιR)\mathfrak{R} = (\kappa_R, N_R, \iota_R):

R=(κR:CapabilitySignature,  NR:Set(Norm),  ιR:CapabilitySignature.SubsumptionMorphism)\mathfrak{R} = (\kappa_R : \mathrm{CapabilitySignature},\; N_R : \mathrm{Set(Norm)},\; \iota_R : \mathrm{CapabilitySignature.SubsumptionMorphism})

where:

  • κR\kappa_R is the capability signature — a morphism κR:ParamNamesR→Types\kappa_R : \mathrm{ParamNames}_R \to \mathrm{Types} (see CapabilitySignature) specifying the minimum typed interface any bearer of RR must provide; agent aa fills RR iff the satisfaction proposition sa,κR∈Ht∗s_{a,\kappa_R} \in H^*_t — aa provides a doubly-settled value for every parameter in κR\kappa_R; in a rule-based system, κR\kappa_R corresponds to the parameter block of the role’s specification
  • NRN_R is the norm-set — the finite set of Norms indexed to role RR; obligations, permissions, and prohibitions that apply to any bearer of RR while they bear it; the norms are role-indexed, not agent-indexed: when aa fills RR, all n∈NRn \in N_R are instantiated with aa as bearer; when aa vacates RR, the norms deactivate for aa and activate for whatever new bearer fills RR; role norms are positional, not personal
  • ιR\iota_R is the subsumption morphism set — the set of subsumption morphisms ι:κR′↪κR\iota : \kappa_{R'} \hookrightarrow \kappa_R (from any less specific role R′R' into RR) making RR a node in the category of capability signatures ordered by subsumption; ι:κR′↪κR\iota : \kappa_{R'} \hookrightarrow \kappa_R exists iff dom(κR′)⊆dom(κR)\mathrm{dom}(\kappa_{R'}) \subseteq \mathrm{dom}(\kappa_R) with matching types; filling RR entails filling every R′R' for which such a morphism exists

Four invariants. R\mathfrak{R} is a role iff it satisfies:

item.title: Law
item.description:
Formal definition

A Law is an element a∈Hta \in H_t satisfying both nuclear fixed-point conditions:

a∈Ht∗=Fix(σt)∩Fix(Δt)a \in H^*_t = \mathrm{Fix}(\sigma_t) \cap \mathrm{Fix}(\Delta_t)

σt(a)=aandΔt(a)=a\sigma_t(a) = a \quad\text{and}\quad \Delta_t(a) = a

By InternalNucleusDefinability, the two conditions are:

  • σ-closure (σt(a)=a\sigma_t(a) = a): aa is the largest element of HtH_t with the same restriction profile to every sub-history. Formally: a=⋁{b∈Ht:∀s≤t, ρs,t(b)=ρs,t(a)}a = \bigvee\{b \in H_t : \forall s \leq t,\ \rho_{s,t}(b) = \rho_{s,t}(a)\}. The accumulated past has pushed aa to its maximum — no sub-history evidence can justify anything higher.
